Issue name: Certain ARIA roles must contain particular children
Issue description: Ensure elements with an ARIA role that require child roles contain them.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
  Element has children which are not allowed: button[tabindex]

Issue name: Buttons must have discernible text
Issue description: Ensure buttons have discernible text.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
  Element does not have inner text that is visible to screen readers
  aria-label attribute does not exist or is empty
  aria-labelledby attribute does not exist, references elements that do not exist or references elements that are empty
  Element has no title attribute
  Element does not have an implicit (wrapped) <label>
  Element does not have an explicit <label>
  Element's default semantics were not overridden with role="none" or role="presentation"

Issue name: Heading levels should only increase by one
Issue description: Ensure the order of headings is semantically correct.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
  Fix the order of headings. Headings should be in descending order of size without skipping levels. For example, <h2> should be followed by <h3>, not <h4> or <h5>.

Issue description: Ensure that link clearly indicates the purpose without requiring additional context. Link text such as "Read more", "Click here", "Learn more" lacks clarity for user using screen reader.
HTML snippet: Learn more
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:

Issue name: Menu must be placed within a landmark region

How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Place the menu within a <nav> landmark. Add role="navigation" or role="menu" to enhance accessibility for the menu.

Issue name: Text intended to be a heading is not identified as one
Issue description: Ensure that text intended to be a heading is properly identified using headers (<h1>, <h2>, etc.) or role="heading".
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:

Issue name: Expandable and collapsible regions must include required ARIA attributes.

Issue name: List structure must not be used where it is not necessary
Issue description: Ensure that elements which are not lists visually are not structured as lists in HTML.
HTML snippet: <div class="fr-list" id="fr-list" role="list" aria-label="Feature requests" aria-live="polite">
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Do not use list attributes (<ul>, <ol> and <li>) for content that is not acting as a list.
A common mistake is to use a new <ul> / <ol> tag for each list item. Ensure that the list hierarchy is well-structured. One <ul> / <ol> tag should pertain to one list level, with multiple <li> elements. A second level can be created by enclosing a <ul>/<ol> in the respective first level <li>.

Issue name: Text intended to be a heading is not identified as one
Issue description: Ensure that text intended to be a heading is properly identified using headers (<h1>, <h2>, etc.) or role="heading".

Fix all of the following:
Use semantic HTML heading tags (<h1> to <h6>) instead of <div> or <span>. 
If using ARIA, apply role="heading" with the appropriate aria-level. 
Ensure that the heading structure follows a logical hierarchy.

Use semantic HTML heading tags (<h1> to <h6>) instead of <div> or <span>. 
If using ARIA, apply role="heading" with the appropriate aria-level. 
Ensure that the heading structure follows a logical hierarchy.

Issue name: Expandable and collapsible regions must include required ARIA attributes.

Fix all of the following:
Use the aria-expanded attribute to indicate the state of a UI element, such as a menu or accordion, showing whether it is expanded or collapsed. For example, aria-expanded="true" signifies that the element is currently expanded, while aria-expanded="false" indicates it is collapsed.

Issue name: Non-text elements must have at least a 3:1 contrast ratio with adjacent colors

 Use sufficient contrast between non-text elements and their background to meet the 3:1 ratio.
Adjust background or border colors to improve contrast for elements like checkboxes, text fields, and icons.
Use high-contrast themes or provide a setting for users to enhance visibility.
Ensure focus indicators, hover states, and active UI elements maintain at least 3:1 contrast.

Issue name: Placeholder is used as a label
Issue description: Ensure that input fields have labels and that placeholders are not the only method used to convey the name or purpose of a form element.
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Always provide a proper visible label for form elements such as text fields, email inputs, and password fields. Do not rely solely on the placeholder attribute to convey the purpose. The placeholder attribute is intended to offer supplemental guidance or examples, not to act as a label. Since it disappears once the user starts typing, it provides no ongoing context, which can confuse screen reader users and impair accessibility.

Issue name: Search functionality must be placed within a landmark region

How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Add a landmark (role="search") to the search functionality on the webpage. Eg: <div role="search"> or <form role="search">

Issue name: Non-text elements must have at least a 3:1 contrast ratio with adjacent colors

Issue name: Text intended to be a heading is not identified as one
Issue description: Ensure that text intended to be a heading is properly identified using headers (<h1>, <h2>, etc.) or role="heading".

Fix all of the following:
Use semantic HTML heading tags (<h1> to <h6>) instead of <div> or <span>.

Ensure that the heading structure follows a logical hierarchy.

Issue name: Menus with dropdowns must include the aria-haspopup attribute
Issue description: Ensure menus with submenus or dropdowns include an aria-haspopup attribute set to true.

Issue name: List structure must not be used where it is not necessary
Issue description: Ensure that elements which are not lists visually are not structured as lists in HTML.
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Do not use list attributes (<ul>, <ol> and <li>) for content that is not acting as a list.
A common mistake is to use a new <ul> / <ol> tag for each list item. Ensure that the list hierarchy is well-structured. One <ul> / <ol> tag should pertain to one list level, with multiple <li> elements. A second level can be created by enclosing a <ul>/<ol> in the respective first level <li>.

Issue name: Interactive elements must be made accessible via keyboard
Issue description: Ensure that interactive elements are focusable to make them keyboard accessible.
HTML snippet: <div class="progress-circle-large" onclick="handlers.showDailyReview()" title="View Productivity Dashboard" style="cursor: pointer;">
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Use native semantic elements whenever possible instead of <div> or <span>. For example, use a <button> for interactive elements instead of a <div> or <span>, as <button> is inherently accessible via keyboard. If a custom implementation is necessary, ensure keyboard accessibility by using the tabindex attribute appropriately.

Issue name: Interactive elements must have a correct role assigned

How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Ensure that a widget role is set on the element. Add the appropriate role from the set of ARIA-prescribed widget roles.
 If the element is composed of multiple interactive elements, add a composite role to the element from the set of composite widget roles set by ARIA.
If the element is not supposed to be interacted with, add a role="none" or role="presentation" to it.

Issue name: Images must have a meaningful alt text
Issue description: Ensure that images have meaningful alt text that serves the same purpose as the image.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Add meaningful alt text to images. Avoid generic phrases like "image," "img," "graph," or "pic" as they do not convey details about the image's content. If an image is purely decorative and does not provide meaningful information, add alt="" or role="presentation" to it. For instance, you can change the alt text for this image from "VaultLister" to our AI-powered suggestion of "VaultLister company logo."

Issue name: Interactive elements must be made accessible via keyboard
Issue description: Ensure that interactive elements are focusable to make them keyboard accessible.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Use native semantic elements whenever possible instead of <div> or <span>. For example, use a <button> for interactive elements instead of a <div> or <span>, as <button> is inherently accessible via keyboard. If a custom implementation is necessary, ensure keyboard accessibility by using the tabindex attribute appropriately.

Issue name: Interactive elements must have a correct role assigned
Issue description: Ensure that the role of an interactive element is not missing or incorrect.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Ensure that a widget role is set on the element. Add the appropriate role from the set of ARIA-prescribed widget roles.
 If the element is composed of multiple interactive elements, add a composite role to the element from the set of composite widget roles set by ARIA.
If the element is not supposed to be interacted with, add a role="none" or role="presentation" to it.

Issue name: Text intended to be a heading is not identified as one

Issue name: Images must have a meaningful alt text
Issue description: Ensure that images have meaningful alt text that serves the same purpose as the image.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Add meaningful alt text to images. Avoid generic phrases like "image," "img," "graph," or "pic" as they do not convey details about the image's content. If an image is purely decorative and does not provide meaningful information, add alt="" or role="presentation" to it. For instance, you can change the alt text for this image from "VaultLister" to our AI-powered suggestion of "VaultLister logo for sign-in page."

Issue name: Non-text elements must have at least a 3:1 contrast ratio with adjacent colors
Issue description: Ensure non-text elements, such as textboxes and checkboxes, meet the minimum 3:1 contrast ratio against surrounding colors.
HTML snippet: <button type="button" id="cookie-accept" style="min-height: 44px; padding: 0.5rem 1.25rem; border-radius: 0.5rem; border: none; background: linear-gradient(135deg, rgb(245, 158, 11), rgb(217, 119, 6)); color: rgb(255, 255, 255); font-size: 0.875rem; font-weight: 600; cursor: pointer;">
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Use sufficient contrast between non-text elements and their background to meet the 3:1 ratio.
Adjust background or border colors to improve contrast for elements like checkboxes, text fields, and icons.
Use high-contrast themes or provide a setting for users to enhance visibility.
Ensure focus indicators, hover states, and active UI elements maintain at least 3:1 contrast.

Issue name: First interactive item in the Web page must be a link to the beginning of the main content
Issue description: Ensure that a mechanism is available to bypass blocks of content that are repeated on multiple Web pages by skipping directly to the main content of the Web page and the element is visible when it receive keyboard focus.
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Add a 'Skip to Main content' link as the first interactive element via keyboard

Issue name: Links must be distinguishable from surrounding text
Issue description: Ensure the links are clearly identifiable from surrounding text using distinct style or colour.
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Add a visual cue for link. Visual cues can be the addition of underlines, font style, bold, or italics, or changes to the font size. Ensure colour contrast between a link and the words around it is greater than 3:1. Further, add visual cue when the user hovers over each link.

Issue name: Elements must have their visible text as part of their accessible name
Issue description: Ensure that elements labelled through their content must have their visible text as part of their accessible name.
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Ensure that the visible label of the element and the accessible name (provided by `aria-label`, etc.) match exactly. Ensure that the visible label of the element is part of the accessible name of the element. It is advisable that the visible label comes in the beginning of the accessible name

Issue name: Text intended to be a heading is not identified as one
Issue description: Ensure that text intended to be a heading is properly identified using headers (<h1>, <h2>, etc.) or role="heading".
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Use semantic HTML heading tags (<h1> to <h6>) instead of <div> or <span>. 
If using ARIA, apply role="heading" with the appropriate aria-level. 
Ensure that the heading structure follows a logical hierarchy.

Issue name: Images must have a meaningful alt text
Issue description: Ensure that images have meaningful alt text that serves the same purpose as the image.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Add meaningful alt text to images. Avoid generic phrases like "image," "img," "graph," or "pic" as they do not convey details about the image's content. If an image is purely decorative and does not provide meaningful information, add alt="" or role="presentation" to it. For instance, you can change the alt text for this image from "VaultLister" to our AI-powered suggestion of "VaultLister company logo."

Issue name: Non-text elements must have at least a 3:1 contrast ratio with adjacent colors
Issue description: Ensure non-text elements, such as textboxes and checkboxes, meet the minimum 3:1 contrast ratio against surrounding colors.
HTML snippet: <button type="button" id="cookie-accept" style="min-height: 44px; padding: 0.5rem 1.25rem; border-radius: 0.5rem; border: none; background: linear-gradient(135deg, rgb(245, 158, 11), rgb(217, 119, 6)); color: rgb(255, 255, 255); font-size: 0.875rem; font-weight: 600; cursor: pointer;">
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Use sufficient contrast between non-text elements and their background to meet the 3:1 ratio.
Adjust background or border colors to improve contrast for elements like checkboxes, text fields, and icons.
Use high-contrast themes or provide a setting for users to enhance visibility.
Ensure focus indicators, hover states, and active UI elements maintain at least 3:1 contrast.

Issue name: Interactive elements must be made accessible via keyboard
Issue description: Ensure that interactive elements are focusable to make them keyboard accessible.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Use native semantic elements whenever possible instead of <div> or <span>. For example, use a <button> for interactive elements instead of a <div> or <span>, as <button> is inherently accessible via keyboard. If a custom implementation is necessary, ensure keyboard accessibility by using the tabindex attribute appropriately.

Issue name: First interactive item in the Web page must be a link to the beginning of the main content
Issue description: Ensure that a mechanism is available to bypass blocks of content that are repeated on multiple Web pages by skipping directly to the main content of the Web page and the element is visible when it receive keyboard focus.
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Add a 'Skip to Main content' link as the first interactive element via keyboard

Issue name: Links must be distinguishable from surrounding text
Issue description: Ensure the links are clearly identifiable from surrounding text using distinct style or colour.
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Add a visual cue for link. Visual cues can be the addition of underlines, font style, bold, or italics, or changes to the font size. Ensure colour contrast between a link and the words around it is greater than 3:1. Further, add visual cue when the user hovers over each link.

Issue name: Elements must have their visible text as part of their accessible name
Issue description: Ensure that elements labelled through their content must have their visible text as part of their accessible name.
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Ensure that the visible label of the element and the accessible name (provided by `aria-label`, etc.) match exactly. Ensure that the visible label of the element is part of the accessible name of the element. It is advisable that the visible label comes in the beginning of the accessible name

Issue name: Text intended to be a heading is not identified as one
Issue description: Ensure that text intended to be a heading is properly identified using headers (<h1>, <h2>, etc.) or role="heading".
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Use semantic HTML heading tags (<h1> to <h6>) instead of <div> or <span>. 
If using ARIA, apply role="heading" with the appropriate aria-level. 
Ensure that the heading structure follows a logical hierarchy.

Issue name: Interactive elements must have a correct role assigned
Issue description: Ensure that the role of an interactive element is not missing or incorrect.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Ensure that a widget role is set on the element. Add the appropriate role from the set of ARIA-prescribed widget roles.
 If the element is composed of multiple interactive elements, add a composite role to the element from the set of composite widget roles set by ARIA.
If the element is not supposed to be interacted with, add a role="none" or role="presentation" to it.

Issue description: Ensure the order of headings is semantically correct.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:

Issue name: First interactive item in the Web page must be a link to the beginning of the main content
Issue description: Ensure that a mechanism is available to bypass blocks of content that are repeated on multiple Web pages by skipping directly to the main content of the Web page and the element is visible when it receive keyboard focus.
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Add a 'Skip to Main content' link as the first interactive element via keyboard

Issue name: Elements must have their visible text as part of their accessible name
Issue description: Ensure that elements labelled through their content must have their visible text as part of their accessible name.
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Ensure that the visible label of the element and the accessible name (provided by `aria-label`, etc.) match exactly. Ensure that the visible label of the element is part of the accessible name of the element. It is advisable that the visible label comes in the beginning of the accessible name

Issue name: Text intended to be a heading is not identified as one
Issue description: Ensure that text intended to be a heading is properly identified using headers (<h1>, <h2>, etc.) or role="heading".
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Use semantic HTML heading tags (<h1> to <h6>) instead of <div> or <span>. 
If using ARIA, apply role="heading" with the appropriate aria-level. 
Ensure that the heading structure follows a logical hierarchy.

Issue name: Interactive elements must have a correct role assigned
Issue description: Ensure that the role of an interactive element is not missing or incorrect.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Ensure that a widget role is set on the element. Add the appropriate role from the set of ARIA-prescribed widget roles.
 If the element is composed of multiple interactive elements, add a composite role to the element from the set of composite widget roles set by ARIA.
If the element is not supposed to be interacted with, add a role="none" or role="presentation" to it.

How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Use sufficient contrast between non-text elements and their background to meet the 3:1 ratio.
Adjust background or border colors to improve contrast for elements like checkboxes, text fields, and icons.
Use high-contrast themes or provide a setting for users to enhance visibility.
Ensure focus indicators, hover states, and active UI elements maintain at least 3:1 contrast.

Issue name: Links must be distinguishable from surrounding text

How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Add a visual cue for link. Visual cues can be the addition of underlines, font style, bold, or italics, or changes to the font size. Ensure colour contrast between a link and the words around it is greater than 3:1. Further, add visual cue when the user hovers over each link.

Issue name: Decorative images must be marked as such
Issue description: Ensure that an image that exists only for aesthetic purposes is not read by assistive technologies.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Add alt="" or role="presentation" to the image. In case of an svg, remove the <title> tag inside the svg, if it exists. Remove role="img" and add role="presentation".

Issue name: Links must be distinguishable from surrounding text
Issue description: Ensure the links are clearly identifiable from surrounding text using distinct style or colour.
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Add a visual cue for link. Visual cues can be the addition of underlines, font style, bold, or italics, or changes to the font size. Ensure colour contrast between a link and the words around it is greater than 3:1. Further, add visual cue when the user hovers over each link.

Issue name: Non-text elements must have at least a 3:1 contrast ratio with adjacent colors
Issue description: Ensure non-text elements, such as textboxes and checkboxes, meet the minimum 3:1 contrast ratio against surrounding colors.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Use sufficient contrast between non-text elements and their background to meet the 3:1 ratio.
Adjust background or border colors to improve contrast for elements like checkboxes, text fields, and icons.
Use high-contrast themes or provide a setting for users to enhance visibility.
Ensure focus indicators, hover states, and active UI elements maintain at least 3:1 contrast.

Issue name: Interactive elements must be made accessible via keyboard
Issue description: Ensure that interactive elements are focusable to make them keyboard accessible.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Use native semantic elements whenever possible instead of <div> or <span>. For example, use a <button> for interactive elements instead of a <div> or <span>, as <button> is inherently accessible via keyboard. If a custom implementation is necessary, ensure keyboard accessibility by using the tabindex attribute appropriately.

Issue name: Interactive elements must have a correct role assigned
Issue description: Ensure that the role of an interactive element is not missing or incorrect.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Ensure that a widget role is set on the element. Add the appropriate role from the set of ARIA-prescribed widget roles.
 If the element is composed of multiple interactive elements, add a composite role to the element from the set of composite widget roles set by ARIA.
If the element is not supposed to be interacted with, add a role="none" or role="presentation" to it.

Issue name: Images must have a meaningful alt text
Issue description: Ensure that images have meaningful alt text that serves the same purpose as the image.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Add meaningful alt text to images. Avoid generic phrases like "image," "img," "graph," or "pic" as they do not convey details about the image's content. If an image is purely decorative and does not provide meaningful information, add alt="" or role="presentation" to it. For instance, you can change the alt text for this image from "VaultLister" to our AI-powered suggestion of "VaultLister logo, e-commerce listing platform."

If the element is not supposed to be interacted with, add a role="none" or role="presentation" to it.

Issue name: Images must have a meaningful alt text
Issue description: Ensure that images have meaningful alt text that serves the same purpose as the image.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Add meaningful alt text to images. Avoid generic phrases like "image," "img," "graph," or "pic" as they do not convey details about the image's content. If an image is purely decorative and does not provide meaningful information, add alt="" or role="presentation" to it. For instance, you can change the alt text for this image from "VaultLister" to our AI-powered suggestion of "VaultLister logo for the reseller platform."

Issue name: Links must be distinguishable from surrounding text
Issue description: Ensure the links are clearly identifiable from surrounding text using distinct style or colour.
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Add a visual cue for link. Visual cues can be the addition of underlines, font style, bold, or italics, or changes to the font size. Ensure colour contrast between a link and the words around it is greater than 3:1. Further, add visual cue when the user hovers over each link.

Issue name: Images must have a meaningful alt text
Issue description: Ensure that images have meaningful alt text that serves the same purpose as the image.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Add meaningful alt text to images. Avoid generic phrases like "image," "img," "graph," or "pic" as they do not convey details about the image's content. If an image is purely decorative and does not provide meaningful information, add alt="" or role="presentation" to it. For instance, you can change the alt text for this image from "VaultLister" to our AI-powered suggestion of "VaultLister brand logo."

Issue name: First interactive item in the Web page must be a link to the beginning of the main content
Issue description: Ensure that a mechanism is available to bypass blocks of content that are repeated on multiple Web pages by skipping directly to the main content of the Web page and the element is visible when it receive keyboard focus.
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Add a 'Skip to Main content' link as the first interactive element via keyboard

Issue name: Form elements must have their visible label as part of their accessible name
Issue description: Ensure that form elements have their visible labels as part of their accessible name.
HTML snippet: <input type="email" id="subscribe-email" placeholder="you@example.com" required="" autocomplete="email" aria-label="Email address">
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Ensure that the form element's visible label (label seen by a visual user) and its accessible name (provided by `aria-label`, etc.) match exactly. Ensure that the form element's visible label is part of its accessible name. It is advisable that the visible label comes in the beginning of the accessible name.

Issue name: Menus with dropdowns must include the aria-haspopup attribute
Issue description: Ensure menus with submenus or dropdowns include an aria-haspopup attribute set to true.
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Add aria-haspopup="true" to menu items with dropdowns. If you can't see the popup on interaction, try to change the dimensions of the page to those of a mobile device and check again. Ensure the menu element with aria-haspopup is focusable and can activate the popup.

How to fix this issue:

Issue name: Text intended to be a heading is not identified as one
Issue description: Ensure that text intended to be a heading is properly identified using headers (<h1>, <h2>, etc.) or role="heading".
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Use semantic HTML heading tags (<h1> to <h6>) instead of <div> or <span>. 
If using ARIA, apply role="heading" with the appropriate aria-level. 
Ensure that the heading structure follows a logical hierarchy.

Issue name: List structure must not be used where it is not necessary
Issue description: Ensure that elements which are not lists visually are not structured as lists in HTML.
HTML snippet: • No issues in the past 90 days.
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Do not use list attributes (<ul>, <ol> and <li>) for content that is not acting as a list.
A common mistake is to use a new <ul> / <ol> tag for each list item. Ensure that the list hierarchy is well-structured. One <ul> / <ol> tag should pertain to one list level, with multiple <li> elements. A second level can be created by enclosing a <ul>/<ol> in the respective first level <li>.

Fix all of the following:
Use semantic HTML heading tags (<h1> to <h6>) instead of <div> or <span>. 
If using ARIA, apply role="heading" with the appropriate aria-level. 
Ensure that the heading structure follows a logical hierarchy.

Issue name: Elements must have their visible text as part of their accessible name
Issue description: Ensure that elements labelled through their content must have their visible text as part of their accessible name.
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Ensure that the visible label of the element and the accessible name (provided by `aria-label`, etc.) match exactly. Ensure that the visible label of the element is part of the accessible name of the element. It is advisable that the visible label comes in the beginning of the accessible name

Issue name: Images must have a meaningful alt text
Issue description: Ensure that images have meaningful alt text that serves the same purpose as the image.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Add meaningful alt text to images. Avoid generic phrases like "image," "img," "graph," or "pic" as they do not convey details about the image's content. If an image is purely decorative and does not provide meaningful information, add alt="" or role="presentation" to it. For instance, you can change the alt text for this image from "Whatnot" to our AI-powered suggestion of "Whatnot company icon logo."

Issue name: Decorative images must be marked as such
Issue description: Ensure that an image that exists only for aesthetic purposes is not read by assistive technologies.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Add alt="" or role="presentation" to the image. In case of an svg, remove the <title> tag inside the svg, if it exists. Remove role="img" and add role="presentation".

Issue name: Non-text elements must have at least a 3:1 contrast ratio with adjacent colors
Issue description: Ensure non-text elements, such as textboxes and checkboxes, meet the minimum 3:1 contrast ratio against surrounding colors.
HTML snippet: <button type="button" id="cookie-accept" style="min-height: 44px; padding: 0.5rem 1.25rem; border-radius: 0.5rem; border: none; background: linear-gradient(135deg, rgb(245, 158, 11), rgb(217, 119, 6)); color: rgb(255, 255, 255); font-size: 0.875rem; font-weight: 600; cursor: pointer;">
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Use sufficient contrast between non-text elements and their background to meet the 3:1 ratio.
Adjust background or border colors to improve contrast for elements like checkboxes, text fields, and icons.
Use high-contrast themes or provide a setting for users to enhance visibility.
Ensure focus indicators, hover states, and active UI elements maintain at least 3:1 contrast.

Issue name: Non-text elements must have at least a 3:1 contrast ratio with adjacent colors
Issue description: Ensure non-text elements, such as textboxes and checkboxes, meet the minimum 3:1 contrast ratio against surrounding colors.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Use sufficient contrast between non-text elements and their background to meet the 3:1 ratio.
Adjust background or border colors to improve contrast for elements like checkboxes, text fields, and icons.
Use high-contrast themes or provide a setting for users to enhance visibility.
Ensure focus indicators, hover states, and active UI elements maintain at least 3:1 contrast.

Issue name: Images must have a meaningful alt text
Issue description: Ensure that images have meaningful alt text that serves the same purpose as the image.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Add meaningful alt text to images. Avoid generic phrases like "image," "img," "graph," or "pic" as they do not convey details about the image's content. If an image is purely decorative and does not provide meaningful information, add alt="" or role="presentation" to it. For instance, you can change the alt text for this image from "VaultLister" to our AI-powered suggestion of "VaultLister logo."

How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Add a 'Skip to Main content' link as the first interactive element via keyboard

Issue name: Elements must have their visible text as part of their accessible name
Issue description: Ensure that elements labelled through their content must have their visible text as part of their accessible name.

Issue name: Text intended to be a heading is not identified as one
Issue description: Ensure that text intended to be a heading is properly identified using headers (<h1>, <h2>, etc.) or role="heading".
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Use semantic HTML heading tags (<h1> to <h6>) instead of <div> or <span>. 
If using ARIA, apply role="heading" with the appropriate aria-level. 
Ensure that the heading structure follows a logical hierarchy.

If the element is not supposed to be interacted with, add a role="none" or role="presentation" to it.

Issue name: Images must have a meaningful alt text
Issue description: Ensure that images have meaningful alt text that serves the same purpose as the image.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Add meaningful alt text to images. Avoid generic phrases like "image," "img," "graph," or "pic" as they do not convey details about the image's content. If an image is purely decorative and does not provide meaningful information, add alt="" or role="presentation" to it. For instance, you can change the alt text for this image from "VaultLister" to our AI-powered suggestion of "VaultLister logo and homepage branding."

Issue name: Non-text elements must have at least a 3:1 contrast ratio with adjacent colors
Issue description: Ensure non-text elements, such as textboxes and checkboxes, meet the minimum 3:1 contrast ratio against surrounding colors.
HTML snippet: <button type="button" id="cookie-accept" style="min-height: 44px; padding: 0.5rem 1.25rem; border-radius: 0.5rem; border: none; background: linear-gradient(135deg, rgb(245, 158, 11), rgb(217, 119, 6)); color: rgb(255, 255, 255); font-size: 0.875rem; font-weight: 600; cursor: pointer;">
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Use sufficient contrast between non-text elements and their background to meet the 3:1 ratio.
Adjust background or border colors to improve contrast for elements like checkboxes, text fields, and icons.
Use high-contrast themes or provide a setting for users to enhance visibility.
Ensure focus indicators, hover states, and active UI elements maintain at least 3:1 contrast.

Issue name: Interactive elements must be made accessible via keyboard
Issue description: Ensure that interactive elements are focusable to make them keyboard accessible.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Use native semantic elements whenever possible instead of <div> or <span>. For example, use a <button> for interactive elements instead of a <div> or <span>, as <button> is inherently accessible via keyboard. If a custom implementation is necessary, ensure keyboard accessibility by using the tabindex attribute appropriately.

Addition or removal of items are not violations, only the ones which are present across web pages need to follow the same order of appearance in every web page.

Issue name: Interactive elements must be made accessible via keyboard
Issue description: Ensure that interactive elements are focusable to make them keyboard accessible.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Use native semantic elements whenever possible instead of <div> or <span>. For example, use a <button> for interactive elements instead of a <div> or <span>, as <button> is inherently accessible via keyboard. If a custom implementation is necessary, ensure keyboard accessibility by using the tabindex attribute appropriately.

How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Add a 'Skip to Main content' link as the first interactive element via keyboard

Issue name: Links must be distinguishable from surrounding text
Issue description: Ensure the links are clearly identifiable from surrounding text using distinct style or colour.
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Add a visual cue for link. Visual cues can be the addition of underlines, font style, bold, or italics, or changes to the font size. Ensure colour contrast between a link and the words around it is greater than 3:1. Further, add visual cue when the user hovers over each link.

Issue name: Elements must have their visible text as part of their accessible name
Issue description: Ensure that elements labelled through their content must have their visible text as part of their accessible name.
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Ensure that the visible label of the element and the accessible name (provided by `aria-label`, etc.) match exactly. Ensure that the visible label of the element is part of the accessible name of the element. It is advisable that the visible label comes in the beginning of the accessible name

Issue name: Text intended to be a heading is not identified as one
Issue description: Ensure that text intended to be a heading is properly identified using headers (<h1>, <h2>, etc.) or role="heading".
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Use semantic HTML heading tags (<h1> to <h6>) instead of <div> or <span>. 
If using ARIA, apply role="heading" with the appropriate aria-level. 
Ensure that the heading structure follows a logical hierarchy.

Issue name: Non-text elements must have at least a 3:1 contrast ratio with adjacent colors
Issue description: Ensure non-text elements, such as textboxes and checkboxes, meet the minimum 3:1 contrast ratio against surrounding colors.
HTML snippet: <button type="button" id="cookie-accept" style="min-height: 44px; padding: 0.5rem 1.25rem; border-radius: 0.5rem; border: none; background: linear-gradient(135deg, rgb(245, 158, 11), rgb(217, 119, 6)); color: rgb(255, 255, 255); font-size: 0.875rem; font-weight: 600; cursor: pointer;">
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Use sufficient contrast between non-text elements and their background to meet the 3:1 ratio.
Adjust background or border colors to improve contrast for elements like checkboxes, text fields, and icons.
Use high-contrast themes or provide a setting for users to enhance visibility.
Ensure focus indicators, hover states, and active UI elements maintain at least 3:1 contrast.

How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Use semantic HTML heading tags (<h1> to <h6>) instead of <div> or <span>. 
If using ARIA, apply role="heading" with the appropriate aria-level. 
Ensure that the heading structure follows a logical hierarchy.

Issue name: Images must have a meaningful alt text
Issue description: Ensure that images have meaningful alt text that serves the same purpose as the image.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Add meaningful alt text to images. Avoid generic phrases like "image," "img," "graph," or "pic" as they do not convey details about the image's content. If an image is purely decorative and does not provide meaningful information, add alt="" or role="presentation" to it. For instance, you can change the alt text for this image from "VaultLister" to our AI-powered suggestion of "VaultLister brand logo for task management platform."

Issue name: Elements must have their visible text as part of their accessible name
Issue description: Ensure that elements labelled through their content must have their visible text as part of their accessible name.
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Ensure that the visible label of the element and the accessible name (provided by `aria-label`, etc.) match exactly. Ensure that the visible label of the element is part of the accessible name of the element. It is advisable that the visible label comes in the beginning of the accessible name

Issue name: Interactive elements must be made accessible via keyboard
Issue description: Ensure that interactive elements are focusable to make them keyboard accessible.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Use native semantic elements whenever possible instead of <div> or <span>. For example, use a <button> for interactive elements instead of a <div> or <span>, as <button> is inherently accessible via keyboard. If a custom implementation is necessary, ensure keyboard accessibility by using the tabindex attribute appropriately.

Issue name: Fields with asterisks should have an instruction that indicates them as required
Issue description: Ensure that users understand that an asterisk on a field indicates a required field.
HTML snippet: <textarea id="apply-plan" name="promotion_plan" required="" placeholder="e.g. YouTube reviews, Instagram Reels, reselling community newsletter…"></textarea>
How to fix this issue:
Fix all of the following:
Ensure a description is included in the field's label or related instructions that explicitly states that an asterisk indicates that the field is required.

Issue name: Non-text elements must have at least a 3:1 contrast ratio with adjacent colors
Issue description: Ensure non-text elements, such as textboxes and checkboxes, meet the minimum 3:1 contrast ratio against surrounding colors.
HTML snippet: <button type="button" id="cookie-accept" style="min-height: 44px; padding: 0.5rem 1.25rem; border-radius: 0.5rem; border: none; background: linear-gradient(135deg, rgb(245, 158, 11), rgb(217, 119, 6)); color: rgb(255, 255, 255); font-size: 0.875rem; font-weight: 600; cursor: pointer;">
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Use sufficient contrast between non-text elements and their background to meet the 3:1 ratio.
Adjust background or border colors to improve contrast for elements like checkboxes, text fields, and icons.
Use high-contrast themes or provide a setting for users to enhance visibility.
Ensure focus indicators, hover states, and active UI elements maintain at least 3:1 contrast.

Issue name: Interactive elements must have a correct role assigned
Issue description: Ensure that the role of an interactive element is not missing or incorrect.
How to fix this issue:
Fix any of the following:
 Ensure that a widget role is set on the element. Add the appropriate role from the set of ARIA-prescribed widget roles.
 If the element is composed of multiple interactive elements, add a composite role to the element from the set of composite widget roles set by ARIA.
If the element is not supposed to be interacted with, add a role="none" or role="presentation" to it.
